policy_statement: Chicago will reduce citywide greenhouse gas emissions by at least 62% by 2040 from the 2017 baseline.
verbatim_text: The 2022 CAP aims to chart an equitable path to reduce Chicago's GHG emissions by a minimum of 62% by 2040. Following the principles defined in this plan, this pathway prioritizes improving the lives of all Chicagoans by promoting environmental justice, generating household savings, improving community health, transitioning away from fossil-fuel dependency, and ensuring that all Chicago communities are prepared for the undeniable impacts of climate change, by 2040.
section_header: GHG REDUCTION Chicago's TARGETS
preceding_paragraph: The 2022 CAP aims to chart an equitable path to reduce Chicago's GHG emissions by a minimum of 62% by 2040. Following the principles defined in this plan, this pathway prioritizes improving the lives of all Chicagoans by promoting environmental justice, generating household savings, improving community health, transitioning away from fossil-fuel dependency, and ensuring that all Chicago communities are prepared for the undeniable impacts of climate change, by 2040.
policy_group_name: GHG REDUCTION TARGETS
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 'reduce Chicago's GHG emissions by a minimum of 62% by 2040'.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago (citywide).
has_implementation_pathway: No. The sentence states the target; implementation is outlined elsewhere in CAP but not in this statement.
extraction_rationale: City-wide GHG reduction target with a firm percentage and deadline; core commitment of the plan.

policy_statement: Retrofit residential buildings with 4 or fewer units to achieve 20% coverage by 2030 and 50% by 2040, prioritizing low- or moderate-income households.
verbatim_text: Retrofit residential buildings with 4 or fewer units: 20% by 2030 and 50% by 2040, prioritizing low- or moderate-income households. Smaller residential buildings, including the city's nearly 400,000 single-family homes and small multifamily properties, generated 14% of the city's total carbon emissions in 2017. To reach the City's 2030 goal, 78,500 smaller residential buildings must be retrofitted, and to reach the City's 2050 goal, 196,000 smaller residential buildings must be retrofitted.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households > STRATEGY 1 RETROFIT BUILDINGS
preceding_paragraph: Chicago's residential, commercial, and industrial buildings account for nearly 69% of the City's carbon footprint. Reducing building energy use through efficiency will reduce the city's GHG emissions, save residents and businesses money, and improve the city's resiliency to climate shocks and stressors. Upgraded technologies that deliver reliable efficiency outcomes within reasonable payback periods are readily available, making scaled implementation of available technology a priority for the many buildings that have not yet undergone retrofits.
policy_group_name: Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households — Strategy 1: Retrofit buildings
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Retrofit 20% by 2030 and 50% by 2040.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago (citywide residential buildings with 4 or fewer units).
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Implemented under Pillar 1 Strategy 1 with City Partners (CHA, City Treasurer, DOB, DOH, DPD) and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Quantified retrofit target with deadlines, prioritized beneficiaries, and an action under a named strategy with City Partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Retrofit 20% of total 5+ unit residential buildings by 2030.
verbatim_text: Retrofit 20% of total 5+ unit residential buildings by 2030. This action will bring improved efficiency and living conditions to nearly 140,000 units in large multifamily buildings. Utility costs are among the largest operating expenses for multifamily buildings in Chicago, and this portion of Chicago's housing stock accounted for 12.9% of the city's total carbon footprint in 2017.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households > STRATEGY 1 RETROFIT BUILDINGS
preceding_paragraph: Retrofit implementation strategies vary by building type and each building's unique needs. Retrofits for most building types can include weatherization, smart thermostats, lighting and appliance replacements, and heating, ventilation, and air conditioning equipment upgrades. Industrial buildings that use energy for manufacturing must explore efficiency opportunities specific to their specialized equipment and processes.
policy_group_name: Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households — Strategy 1: Retrofit buildings
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Retrofit 20% of 5+ unit residential buildings by 2030.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Action under Strategy 1 with City Partners (CHA, DOB, DOH, DPD) and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Quantified target with deadline under a named strategy; includes City Partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Retrofit 90% of total City-owned and sister agency-owned buildings by 2035.
verbatim_text: Retrofit 90% of total city-owned and sister agency-owned buildings by 2035. City and sister agency buildings include public schools, community centers, libraries, fire and police stations, offices, and other buildings that serve the public. Although they generated only 1% of the city's total carbon footprint in 2017, it is important for the City to lead by example and bring energy efficiency technology to its own facilities.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households > STRATEGY 1 RETROFIT BUILDINGS
preceding_paragraph: It is important to prioritize energy efficiency retrofits prior to electrification and the transition to renewable energy to lower overall costs and maximize savings. In addition, these investments make buildings healthier and more comfortable for people. Efficiency enables the transition to 100% renewable energy by reducing the amount of new wind and solar power required to meet all energy needs.
policy_group_name: Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households — Strategy 1: Retrofit buildings
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Retrofit 90% by 2035.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City-owned and sister agency-owned buildings in Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Action under Strategy 1 with extensive City Partners (AIS, CCC, CDA, CDOT, CPD, CPS, CTA, DOB, DPD, DWM, PBC).
extraction_rationale: Quantified public sector retrofit target and deadline; specific action with City Partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Install 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects within Chicago by 2025, prioritizing households with the highest energy burden.
verbatim_text: Install 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects by 2025. By 2025, 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects will be installed within city limits, prioritizing households with the highest energy burden. This amount of solar can power more than 1,100 Chicago homes each year.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households > STRATEGY 2 CONNECT COMMUNITIES TO RENEWABLE ENERGY
preceding_paragraph: Expanding innovative community ownership models for renewable energy projects on City property and utilizing state funding will help Chicago achieve 100% clean renewable energy community-wide by 2035. The City will support the development of renewable energy generation to bring the economic and pollution-reduction benefits of renewable energy to Chicago homes and businesses. Connection to renewable energy can increase community resiliency and decrease household energy costs.
policy_group_name: Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households — Strategy 2: Connect communities to renewable energy
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 5 MW by 2025.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Within city limits; prioritizing high energy-burden households.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Strategy 2 with City Partners (AIS, CCC, CHA, CPS, DOB, DPD) and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Quantified renewable energy deployment with deadline; clear action within a named strategy and city partners.

policy_statement: Increase Chicago-based community renewables to 20 megawatts by 2025.
verbatim_text: Increase Chicago-based community renewables to 20 megawatts by 2025. Expanding access to renewable energy for all Chicagoans starts by building energy infrastructure within the city limits. Community renewables provide all Chicagoans access to the benefits of renewable generation, including lower electric bills, even if they are unable to have an on-site solar system at their home or business.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households > STRATEGY 2 CONNECT COMMUNITIES TO RENEWABLE ENERGY
preceding_paragraph: Those making decisions about clean-energy approaches should consider these initial questions to improve the equity, resiliency, and environmental justice outcomes of their efforts:
policy_group_name: Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households — Strategy 2: Connect communities to renewable energy
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 20 MW by 2025.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Chicago-based community renewables.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Implemented under Strategy 2 with City Partners (BACP, DFSS, DPD).
extraction_rationale: Quantified city-based community renewable capacity target with deadline; specific action with partners.

policy_statement: Increase community renewable subscriptions so that 25% of subscriptions are held by low-income and/or environmental justice low-income residents by 2030.
verbatim_text: Increase community renewables subscriptions to achieve 25% subscribed by low-income and/ or environmental justice low-income residents by 2030. Providing access to community renewables for low-income or environmental justice households reduces energy cost burdens.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households > STRATEGY 2 CONNECT COMMUNITIES TO RENEWABLE ENERGY
preceding_paragraph: Expanding innovative community ownership models for renewable energy projects on City property and utilizing state funding will help Chicago achieve 100% clean renewable energy community-wide by 2035. The City will support the development of renewable energy generation to bring the economic and pollution-reduction benefits of renewable energy to Chicago homes and businesses.
policy_group_name: Pillar 1: Increase access to utility savings and renewable energy, prioritizing households — Strategy 2: Connect communities to renewable energy
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 25% subscription by 2030 for low-income/EJ residents.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago households, with emphasis on low-income and environmental justice communities.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Implemented under Strategy 2 with City Partners (BACP, DFSS, DPD) and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Quantified equity-focused subscription target with deadline; implemented under a named strategy and partners.

policy_statement: Introduce community-wide organic waste collection and decomposition across Chicago by 2025.
verbatim_text: Introduce community-wide organic waste collection and decomposition by 2025. In households and restaurants, food waste is typically the largest portion of the waste stream. When sent to landfills, food waste causes detrimental methane emissions.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 2: Build circular economies to create jobs and reduce waste > STRATEGY 1 REDUCE WASTE AND LANDFILLING
preceding_paragraph: Acknowledging that reducing overall waste provides the greatest benefits, it is also important to improve the convenience and effectiveness of waste management infrastructure, so that it is easier for individuals and businesses to divert waste from landfills. Reusing durable materials for as long as possible, improving recycling rates and addressing contamination issues, and composting organic waste are key starting points.
policy_group_name: Pillar 2: Build circular economies to create jobs and reduce waste — Strategy 1: Reduce waste and landfilling
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Citywide program established by 2025.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide (community-wide collection and decomposition).
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Implemented under Strategy 1 with City Partners (CDPH, CPS, DPD, DSS) and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Citywide organics program with deadline; concrete intervention under waste strategy with partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Divert 90% of commercial, industrial, and institutional waste by 2030.
verbatim_text: Divert 90% of commercial, industrial, and institutional waste by 2030. Between 2010 and 2020, Chicago's commercial and industrial waste increased from 1.2 million tons in 2010 to almost 1.5 million tons, with most of this waste ending up in landfills. To achieve a 90% diversion goal by 2030, the City will help commercial and industrial businesses implement new source reduction and prevention techniques and improve reuse and recycling.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 2: Build circular economies to create jobs and reduce waste > STRATEGY 1 REDUCE WASTE AND LANDFILLING
preceding_paragraph:
KNOW HURDLES
-  Lack of institutional capacity within city and budget for transitioning from black cart system to more effective options
-  Promoting and evaluating the success of prevention approaches
-  Communicating materials management procedures effectively with diverse audiences
-  Complexity of regulatory structures
policy_group_name: Pillar 2: Build circular economies to create jobs and reduce waste — Strategy 1: Reduce waste and landfilling
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 90% diversion by 2030.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide sectors (commercial, industrial, institutional).
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Implemented under waste strategy with City Partners (CDPH, DOB, DPD, DSS).
extraction_rationale: Aggressive diversion target with deadline for commercial/industrial/institutional waste; specific action with partners.

policy_statement: Divert 90% of residential waste by 2040.
verbatim_text: Divert 90% of residential waste by 2040. Smaller residential buildings in Chicago are currently served by the City's Blue and Black Cart Programs for recycling and garbage. In both cases, access to composting services is rare unless individuals go out of their way to find options. This patchwork of practices creates challenges and can be improved by standardizing recycling practices and expanding composting options.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 2: Build circular economies to create jobs and reduce waste > STRATEGY 1 REDUCE WASTE AND LANDFILLING
preceding_paragraph:
Department of Streets and Sanitation Launches Community-Wide Composting
Leveraging the support from the Natural Resources Defense  Council's  Food  Matters  Program,  the Department of Streets and Sanitation (DSS) will launch five community organic waste drop-off sites located at NeighborSpace community gardens.
policy_group_name: Pillar 2: Build circular economies to create jobs and reduce waste — Strategy 1: Reduce waste and landfilling
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 90% diversion by 2040.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide residential waste stream.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under waste strategy with City Partners and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Residential diversion target with deadline; concrete action under waste strategy with partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Expand high-quality, low-stress on-street bikeways and off-street trails by adding 25 miles of protected bike lanes and 25 miles of neighborhood greenways and eliminating 15 miles of bikeway network gaps by the end of 2024.
verbatim_text: Expand high-quality and low-stress on-street bikeways and off-street trails. Through the Chicago Works program, the City has significantly increased its construction of low-stress bikeways, including both protected bike lanes and neighborhood greenways. To continue improving community mobility and support citywide transportation emissions reductions, by the end of 2024 the City aims to add 25 more miles of protected bike lanes, 25 more miles of neighborhood greenways, and meet the CDOT Strategic Plan goal of eliminating 15 miles of gaps in the citywide bikeways network.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network that connects communities and improves air quality > STRATEGY 1 MAKE WALKING, BIKING, OR TRANSIT VIABLE OPTIONS FOR ALL TRIPS
preceding_paragraph: Continuing to make cycling a greater part of how we get around in Chicago requires a biking network that serves all neighborhoods and makes everyday bicycling safe and convenient for people of all ages and abilities. Biking must feel safe, in addition to being statistically safe. The City aims to make cyclists feel safe and experience a low level of traffic stress.
policy_group_name: Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network — Strategy 1: Make walking, biking, or transit viable options for all trips
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Add 25 miles protected bike lanes, 25 miles neighborhood greenways by end of 2024; eliminate 15 miles of gaps.
has_timeline: Yes. By end of 2024; further by 2030 (bike rate doubling).
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago street and trail network.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Part of Strategy 1 with CDOT/DPD; details on miles of lanes and network gaps.
extraction_rationale: Specific infrastructure buildout goals with deadlines; clear implementation under transport strategy with partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Increase Divvy and shared micromobility trips by 30% by 2030.
verbatim_text: Increase Divvy bikes and shared micromobility trips 30% by 2030. The City targets achieving a ridership of at least 1.5 Divvy bike trips per day from May to October for every thousand residents in five economic hardship areas (identified via census and public health data). Increasing Divvy bike ridership to reduce vehicle miles traveled by 2.2 million miles will improve city air quality, public health, and reduce citywide transportation emissions.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network that connects communities and improves air quality > STRATEGY 1 MAKE WALKING, BIKING, OR TRANSIT VIABLE OPTIONS FOR ALL TRIPS
preceding_paragraph:
KNOW HURDLES
-  Time-intensive nature of network planning to seek meaningful input from community stakeholders
-  Funding for total system completion
-  Acceptance of bike lanes
-  Sidewalk quality data
-  Biking network, while in progress, has gaps
policy_group_name: Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network — Strategy 1: Make walking, biking, or transit viable options for all trips
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 30% increase by 2030.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide Divvy/shared micromobility.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Strategy outlines bus priority treatments and infrastructure investments with CDOT/CTA/DPD.
extraction_rationale: Quantified mode-share growth target with deadline; clear ridership/mode metrics and supporting measures.

policy_statement: Enable Chicagoans to walk, bike, take transit, or use shared micromobility for 45% of all trips by 2040, supported by bus priority corridors and active transportation infrastructure.
verbatim_text: Enable Chicagoans to walk, bike, take transit, or use shared micromobility for 45% of all trips by 2040. From 2022 through 2024, the City intends to meet the CDOT Strategic Plan goals of designing and installing bus priority treatments along three corridors. Between 2025 and 2040, the City will continue to set annual benchmarks for infrastructure investments in protected bike lanes, neighborhood greenways, vision zero safety improvements and transit priority treatments.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network that connects communities and improves air quality > STRATEGY 1 MAKE WALKING, BIKING, OR TRANSIT VIABLE OPTIONS FOR ALL TRIPS
preceding_paragraph: To ensure that walking, biking, and transit use remain viable for all trips, the City must prioritize sidewalk and road maintenance by using an equity lens along with condition assessments to address historic imbalances in the upkeep of City infrastructure. Continued coordination between CTA, Metra, and PACE is also critical to enable riders to use all three systems as a seamless transit network.
policy_group_name: Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network — Strategy 1: Make walking, biking, or transit viable options for all trips
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 45% of all trips by 2040 via walk/bike/transit/shared micromobility.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Includes bus priority treatments, protected bike lanes, greenways, Vision Zero improvements with CDOT/CTA/DPD.
extraction_rationale: Citywide travel mode target with deadline; detailed supporting actions and agencies.

policy_statement: Update citywide car and bike parking requirements by 2025 to modernize standards and reduce vehicle miles traveled.
verbatim_text: Update citywide car and bike parking requirements by 2025. The current zoning code parking minimums mandate that new developments build on-site car parking spaces based on 2004 zoning policy standards. These mandates frequently lead to an oversupply of off-street parking, which unnecessarily encourages more residents to drive, increasing vehicle miles traveled and GHG emissions.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network that connects communities and improves air quality > STRATEGY 2 INCREASE TRANSIT PERFORMANCE AND ENCOURAGE EQUITABLE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT
preceding_paragraph:
KNOW HURDLES
-  The ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and shifts to remote working have reduced transit ridership
-  Reliable charging infrastructure and updated maintenance protocols are needed for electric buses
policy_group_name: Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network — Strategy 2: Increase transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development
has_quantifiable_target: No. It’s a policy change with a deadline, not a numeric target.
has_binding_mechanism: No. It is a commitment to update requirements; not yet adopted.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide parking requirements.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Part of Strategy 2 with CDOT/DPD and an adoption timeline.
extraction_rationale: Regulatory update commitment with deadline; specific intervention under transit strategy that will shape land-use and parking supply.

policy_statement: Increase CTA ridership by 20% by 2030, supported by bus priority treatments, TDM plans, and other measures that reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips.
verbatim_text: Increase CTA ridership 20% by 2030. Increasing CTA ridership will require sustained investment in infrastructure and service improvements to make it more competitive to driving and to ensure a convenient, affordable, and safe option for all residents. A suite of policies will be implemented to reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network that connects communities and improves air quality > STRATEGY 2 INCREASE TRANSIT PERFORMANCE AND ENCOURAGE EQUITABLE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT
preceding_paragraph: Whether traveling within your neighborhood or crisscrossing the city for a longer adventure, reliable transit plays a vital role in keeping our city connected. Despite the dramatic decrease in ridership, the COVID-19 pandemic proved that public transit is a critical part of an equitable economy and must be preserved and expanded.
policy_group_name: Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network — Strategy 2: Increase transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 20% increase by 2030 (from 2019 baseline, after rebound).
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. CTA system in Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. References bus priority lanes, TDM plans, and potential congestion pricing with CDOT/CTA/DPD.
extraction_rationale: Quantified public transit ridership target with deadline; includes supporting policy levers.

policy_statement: Support equitable electrification of ride-hail and taxi fleets by 2030, enable 100% electrification of delivery fleets by 2035, and electrify 100% of the City’s fleet by 2035.
verbatim_text: Support equitable electrification of ride-hail and taxi fleets by 2030. Enable 100% electrification of delivery fleets by 2035. Electrify 100% of the City's fleet by 2035.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network that connects communities and improves air quality > STRATEGY 3 ENABLE ZERO-EMISSION TRANSIT AND FLEETS
preceding_paragraph: Shifting to electric taxis, ride hail fleet, and City vehicles will replace approximately 50,000 combustion-engine vehicles. The equitable expansion and distribution of charging stations will enable more residents to benefit from electric vehicle use.
policy_group_name: Pillar 3: Deliver a robust zero-emission mobility network — Strategy 3: Enable zero-emission transit and fleets
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 2030 for ride-hail/taxi support; 2035 for delivery fleets; 2035 for City fleet 100%.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago fleets and services.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Includes development of fleet plans, charging infrastructure focus, and City Partners.
extraction_rationale: Fleet electrification targets with deadlines; specific actions with partners under Strategy 3.

policy_statement: Aggregate 5,000 megawatts of clean renewable energy within a 250-mile radius of Chicago by 2030.
verbatim_text: Aggregate 5,000 megawatts of clean renewable energy within a 250-mile radius of Chicago by 2030. By 2030, Chicago will bolster its relationships with surrounding counties to promote the growth of clean renewable energy, aligned with Illinois' goal of reaching 100% clean energy statewide by 2050.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 1 100% CLEAN RENEWABLE ENERGY
preceding_paragraph: We can make our electric grid 'cleaner' and enhance power reliability by investing in the development of new renewable energy generation sources and storage. Installing and expanding in-city and in-state renewable energy will spur economic growth and create employment opportunities.
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 1: 100% clean renewable energy
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 5,000 MW by 2030 within 250 miles.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Within a 250-mile radius of Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Strategy 1 with AIS/DPD and linkage to Illinois CEJA; performance metrics defined.
extraction_rationale: Regional renewable aggregation target with deadline; concrete action with partners under clean energy strategy.

policy_statement: Install 30 megawatts of clean renewable energy projects on City property by 2030.
verbatim_text: Install 30 megawatts of clean renewable energy projects on City property by 2030. The City will lead by example in the transition to clean energy by taking advantage of its physical assets. Installing solar arrays on City-owned properties will ensure that previously underutilized space can reduce energy costs, increase local job opportunities, and improve citywide energy resiliency.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 1 100% CLEAN RENEWABLE ENERGY
preceding_paragraph: The City is grateful to the C40 team for its guidance that has helped to shape this plan and its ambitions.
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 1: 100% clean renewable energy
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 30 MW by 2030 on City property.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City-owned properties in Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Strategy 1 with City Partners and explicit feasibility assessments and prioritization.
extraction_rationale: On-site clean energy installation target with deadline; specific action with partners under clean energy strategy.

policy_statement: Achieve 100% clean renewable energy community-wide by 2035.
verbatim_text: Achieve 100% clean renewable energy communitywide by 2035. By 2035, the City will work with utilities to provide 100% clean renewable energy community-wide. The City will prioritize local generation and provide communities shared leadership in determining where renewable projects are constructed.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 1 100% CLEAN RENEWABLE ENERGY
preceding_paragraph: Installing and expanding in-city and in-state renewable energy will spur economic growth and create employment opportunities. As the renewable electricity supply grows, highly polluting fossil-fuel electricity plants can be retired and remediated.
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 1: 100% clean renewable energy
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 100% clean renewable energy community-wide by 2035.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Strategy calls for working with utilities, prioritizing local generation, and shared leadership in siting.
extraction_rationale: Citywide clean energy target with deadline; core clean energy commitment.

policy_statement: Enact policies by 2023 that support electrified renovations for all existing building types and all-electric new construction in Chicago.
verbatim_text: Enact policies that support electrified renovations and new construction by 2023. By 2023, the City will enable electrified renovations for all existing building types (residen -tial, commercial, and industrial) and new construction. Enacting electrification incentives will send a strong signal to Chicago's construction workforce that they need to prepare for a rapid transition to all electric appliances and equipment.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 2 ENABLE BUILDING AND PERSONAL VEHICLE ELECTRIFICATION
preceding_paragraph: Many buildings use natural gas to run furnaces, boilers, hot water heaters, cooking ranges, and other equipment and appliances. Diesel is commonly used in the backup generators that help maintain power if the electric grid is down.
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 2: Enable building and personal vehicle electrification
has_quantifiable_target: No. It is a policy change with a deadline, not numeric.
has_binding_mechanism: No. It is a commitment to enact policies by 2023; not yet codified.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City of Chicago building market (existing and new construction).
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Strategy 2 with City Partners, incentives, and signaling to construction workforce.
extraction_rationale: Near-term policy enactment commitment enabling electrification; clear timeline and scope across building types.

policy_statement: Electrify 30% of total existing residential buildings in Chicago by 2035.
verbatim_text: Electrify 30% of total existing residential buildings by 2035. Residential building electrification is one of the most important climate actions Chicagoans can take that also provides immediate health benefits for households. Other residential electrification actions will include replacing existing stoves, dryers, and other appliances with electric technology.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 2 ENABLE BUILDING AND PERSONAL VEHICLE ELECTRIFICATION
preceding_paragraph: Those making decisions about electrification approaches should consider these initial questions to improve the equity, resiliency, and environmental justice outcomes of their efforts:
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 2: Enable building and personal vehicle electrification
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Electrify 30% by 2035.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide residential buildings.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Strategy outlines support for heat pumps, appliance replacement, and pairing with weatherization with City Partners.
extraction_rationale: Quantified building electrification target with deadline; clear scope and approach under a named strategy.

policy_statement: Enable installation of 2,500 new commercial-grade Level 2 public passenger EV charging stations by 2035, prioritizing low- and middle-income communities.
verbatim_text: Enable 2,500 new public passenger electric vehicle charging stations by 2035. In coordination with private and public electric vehicle charger operators and building owners, the City will enable the installation of 2,500 new commercial-grade, Level 2 public passenger electric vehicle charging stations by 2035, with priority given to low- and middle-income communities.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 2 ENABLE BUILDING AND PERSONAL VEHICLE ELECTRIFICATION
preceding_paragraph:
KNOW HURDLES
-  Vendor capabilities, available vendors, and vendor contracting support
-  Affordability protections
-  Lack of scalable technology and system reliability planning
-  Buy America requirements for federal funding
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 2: Enable building and personal vehicle electrification
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 2,500 new Level 2 public passenger EV charging stations by 2035.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide deployment with priority to low- and middle-income communities.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Strategy references coordination with private/public charger operators and prioritization for LMI communities.
extraction_rationale: Quantified public EV charging infrastructure buildout with deadline; concrete action under named strategy with partners.

policy_statement: Ensure 150 megawatts of energy storage capacity by 2025 to enhance grid reliability and reduce peaker plant use.
verbatim_text: Ensure 150 megawatts of energy storage by 2025. Building storage capacity adds grid flexibility and reliability and has air quality benefits. By working with partners to build energy storage, the City can prioritize enhancing reliability in communities particularly susceptible to climate-related power-loss events.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 5 ENABLE INTERCONNECTION AND STORAGE
preceding_paragraph: To ensure that cleaning our electric grid enhances power reliability and resiliency, Chicago will increase energy storage and distribution, and will promote better demand response during periods of peak electricity usage. Community energy resiliency, via renewable energy generation combined with energy storage, can help communities withstand power-loss events.
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 5: Enable interconnection and storage
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 150 MW by 2025.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide deployment to enhance reliability, with focus on vulnerable communities.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Strategy 5 with City Partners (AIS, CCC, CFD, CHA, CPS, DOB, DPD) and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Quantified energy storage capacity target with deadline; concrete grid reliability action with partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Invest in large-scale demand response, targeting 1,000 megawatts initially and scaling to 3,000 megawatts to protect reliability during extreme events and reduce reliance on peaker plants.
verbatim_text: Invest in 1,000 megawatts of demand response by 2024, and 3,000 megawatts by 2035. By achieving 1,000 megawatts of new energy demand reduction capacity by 2030, and increasing to 3,000 megawatts by 2040, Chicagoans will be better protected during extreme weather events, when electrical grids are unable to supply enough power to meet surge in demand.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 5 ENABLE INTERCONNECTION AND STORAGE
preceding_paragraph:
KNOW HURDLES
-  Vendor capabilities, available vendors, and vendor contracting support
-  Equitable ownership and placement of energy storage
-  Integrate equitable interconnection and storage strategies into the City's electricity franchise agreement
-  Engage with utility provider to align on shared energy demand reduction plans and efficiency goals
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 5: Enable interconnection and storage
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 'Invest in 1,000 MW by 2024 and 3,000 MW by 2035' (header) vs '1,000 MW by 2030 and 3,000 MW by 2040' (body).
has_timeline: Yes. Timelines stated, though inconsistent in text.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide demand response capacity benefiting Chicagoans.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Strategy 5 with City Partners and performance metrics.
extraction_rationale: Demand response capacity targets with deadlines; clear grid reliability intervention. Note: internal timeline inconsistency is present between header and paragraph.

policy_statement: Develop a fossil-fuel plants transition strategy by 2024 to guide equitable decommissioning and replacement with storage and renewables.
verbatim_text: Develop a fossil-fuel plants transition strategy by 2024. Transitioning to the use of alternatives such as battery storage and renewable energy will reduce emissions and alleviate the burden of legacy pollution exposure. In addition, the City will work with local, state, and regional partners during implementation of the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act (CEJA) to ensure the benefits of a just energy tran -sition support Chicago residents and businesses as well.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's cleanenergy future > STRATEGY 4 DECOMMISSION FOSSIL POWER
preceding_paragraph: Making our electric grid cleaner requires more than improving access to renewable energy generation or incentivizing demand response. The City must also look to where our energy is being produced and how it is being produced.
policy_group_name: Pillar 4: Drive equitable development of Chicago's clean-energy future — Strategy 4: Decommission fossil power
has_quantifiable_target: No. It’s a strategy document commitment with a deadline.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Chicago-relevant peaker plants/Illinois grid context.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Strategy to develop guidance with AIS and partners and link to CEJA implementation.
extraction_rationale: Transition planning for fossil peaker plants with deadline; necessary step for decommissioning; specific action with partner.

policy_statement: Establish a robust outdoor air quality monitoring network across Chicago by 2025.
verbatim_text: Establish a robust outdoor air quality monitoring network by 2025. Measuring and reporting air quality at various sites such as CTA bus and 'L' stations, schools, or city facilities will demonstrate the impact of the City's climate investments and the modernization of planning policies.
section_header: CLIMATE ACTION STRATEGIES > Pillar 5: Strengthen communities and protect health > STRATEGY 1 COLLECT RELEVANT DATA
preceding_paragraph: Energy burden, water quality, soil quality, and air quality data can be used to measure the equity of climate action across Chicago neighborhoods. Data will be collected by community area, where possible, to show decision-makers where and how their decisions most affect residents.
policy_group_name: Pillar 5: Strengthen communities and protect health — Strategy 1: Collect relevant data
has_quantifiable_target: No. It specifies a network by a deadline, not a numeric count in this excerpt.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Citywide network with placement at CTA stations, schools, or city facilities.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Under Pillar 5 Strategy 1 with City Partners and explicit measurement/reporting approach.
extraction_rationale: Air monitoring network build-out with deadline; concrete public health and data infrastructure action with partners and metrics.

policy_statement: Allocate $46 million to plant 75,000 trees over 5 years in underserved community areas.
verbatim_text: Expand Canopy Coverage — $46 million — Plant 75,000 trees over 5 years in underserved community areas. Expected Co-Benefits: • Reduced pollution burden • Increased home value • Lower energy burden • Improved thermal comfort • Increased carbon sink • Stormwater absorption • Reduced urban heat island.
section_header: CLIMATE FINANCING AND DELIVERY CAPACITY
preceding_paragraph: In October 2021, Chicago's City Council approved $188 million for climate resiliency and mitigation, as part of Mayor Lori Lightfoot's $2.5 billion Chicago Recovery Plan. This historic level of investment toward climate action will provide funding for resilient infrastructure and green workforce development opportunities.
policy_group_name: Chicago Recovery Plan climate investments
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Plant 75,000 trees over 5 years.
has_timeline: Yes. Over 5 years (aligned with 2021 approval; implies through ~2026).
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Underserved community areas in Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Funded through the Chicago Recovery Plan with stated deliverables.
extraction_rationale: Resource allocation with explicit budget, deliverables, and timeline; concrete nature-based solution for resilience and carbon sinks.

policy_statement: Invest $8.3 million to decarbonize City fleet and buildings, prioritizing fleet electrification in pollution-burdened neighborhoods.
verbatim_text: City Fleet and Building Decarbonization — $8.3 million — Invest in municipal fleet electrification combined withelectric vehicle charging. This program will prioritize fleet electrification in pollution-burdened neighborhoods. Expected Co-Benefits: • Reduced emissions from City operations • Procurement opportunities for Minority/ Disadvantaged/Women Business Enterprises (MBE/WBE/DBE/BEPD).
section_header: CLIMATE FINANCING AND DELIVERY CAPACITY
preceding_paragraph: For the first time, awareness, expertise, and funding are aligned to support ambitious climate action in Chicago. The City has allocated budget and operational capacity to achieve the first next steps of the CAP and demonstrate success to solidify climate action as a standard line item in future annual budgets. The initiatives in the tables following are fully funded.
policy_group_name: Chicago Recovery Plan climate investments
has_quantifiable_target: No. Budgeted program; electrification scale described but not a numeric fleet target in this table (separate action sets 2035 goal).
has_timeline: Yes. Implied within Recovery Plan timeframe; program funding committed.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. City operations, prioritizing pollution-burdened neighborhoods in Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Funded under the Chicago Recovery Plan with deliverables and equity prioritization.
extraction_rationale: Resource allocation with budget and clear deliverables for municipal decarbonization; includes prioritization in pollution-burdened neighborhoods.

policy_statement: Provide $5 million to develop community solar on industrial roofs to power at least hundreds of households, prioritizing subscriptions for environmental justice, frontline, and underserved communities near the site(s).
verbatim_text: Community Solar on Industrial Roofs — $5 million — Generate solar energy to power at least hundreds of households via a community solar in an industrial community. Community solar subscriptions to be prioritized in environmental justice, frontline, and/or underserved communities that are in close proximity to the site (s).
section_header: CLIMATE FINANCING AND DELIVERY CAPACITY
preceding_paragraph: In addition to these newly available resources, the Build Back Better framework proposed by the US Congress would further enable the conditions needed to successfully deliver Chicago's CAP.
policy_group_name: Chicago Recovery Plan climate investments
has_quantifiable_target: Partially. Scale is 'hundreds of households' powered (qualitative), and a project type (community solar on industrial roofs).
has_timeline: Yes. Within Recovery Plan period; funding committed.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Within Chicago industrial communities near subscribers.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Funded program via Recovery Plan; specifies prioritization and use case.
extraction_rationale: Resource allocation with budget and deliverables for community-scale solar; addresses energy access equity.

policy_statement: Invest $10 million in low-carbon mobility projects, expanding bike and micromobility infrastructure and walkability in priority communities, and provide 5,000 bikes, helmets, and locks to underserved Chicagoans.
verbatim_text: Low-Carbon Mobility Projects — $10 million — Expansion of bike and micromobility infrastructure and walkability investments in priority communities. This project will provide 5,000 bikes, helmets, and locks to underserved Chicagoans. Expected Co-Benefits: • Reduced emissions from avoided vehicle trips • Increased personal mobility • Procurement opportunities for MBE/WBE/ DBE/BEPDs.
section_header: CLIMATE FINANCING AND DELIVERY CAPACITY
preceding_paragraph: In October 2021, Chicago's City Council approved $188 million for climate resiliency and mitigation, as part of Mayor Lori Lightfoot's $2.5 billion Chicago Recovery Plan.
policy_group_name: Chicago Recovery Plan climate investments
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. Provide 5,000 bikes, helmets, and locks; infrastructure expansion.
has_timeline: Yes. Within Recovery Plan period; funding committed.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Priority communities in Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Funded under Recovery Plan with deliverables and co-benefits.
extraction_rationale: Resource allocation with budget and specific deliverables to expand low-carbon mobility; clear quantified distribution of bikes/gear.

policy_statement: Enforce the Building Energy Benchmarking Ordinance (2013) for buildings ≥50,000 sq ft with annual reporting and triennial data verification, and the 2019 Chicago Energy Rating System requiring public display of energy ratings.
verbatim_text: Benchmarking Ordinance, 2013: This ordinance supports greater transparency on energy usage across the building sector. Existing municipal, commercial, and residential buildings that are 50,000 square feet and larger are called to track energy use, report to the City annually, and verify data accuracy every three years. Chicago's benchmarking program was expanded in 2019 with the Chicago Energy Rating System, which assigns an energy rating to covered buildings based on their reporting and requires buildings to display the rating in a prominent location onsite.
section_header: CLIMATE FINANCING AND DELIVERY CAPACITY
preceding_paragraph: Since 2011, the City has launched building-block policies and initiatives to build greater capacity to deliver on the City's climate targets.
has_quantifiable_target: No numeric target; it is a regulatory requirement.
has_timeline: Yes. Ongoing annual reporting; verification every three years; ordinance dates 2013 and 2019.
has_binding_mechanism: Yes. Legal ordinances adopted in 2013 and expanded in 2019 requiring reporting and posting.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Applies to covered buildings in the City of Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Ordinance mandates tracking, reporting, verification every 3 years, and public display of ratings.
extraction_rationale: Binding ordinance requiring building energy benchmarking and public energy rating; strong regulatory mechanism with scope, frequency, and disclosure requirements.

policy_statement: Enforce the 2020 EV Readiness Ordinance requiring new residential buildings with 5+ units and on-site parking to provide 20% EV-ready spaces, and non-residential buildings with 30+ parking spaces to provide 20% EV-ready spaces.
verbatim_text: EV Readiness Ordinance, 2020: This  ordinance requires new construction of a residential building with 5 or more units and on-site parking to have 20% of parking spaces EV Ready, and in non-residential buildings with 30 or more parking spaces to provide 20% of parking spaces as EV ready.
section_header: CLIMATE FINANCING AND DELIVERY CAPACITY
preceding_paragraph: Since 2011, the City has launched building-block policies and initiatives to build greater capacity to deliver on the City's climate targets.
has_quantifiable_target: Yes. 20% of parking spaces EV-ready in specified new construction.
has_timeline: Yes. Ordinance adopted in 2020; ongoing compliance.
has_binding_mechanism: Yes. Legal ordinance (2020) mandating EV-ready parking in specified new buildings.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes. Applies to new construction in the City of Chicago.
has_implementation_pathway: Yes. Ordinance defines scope and minimum EV-ready parking percentages by building type.
extraction_rationale: Binding ordinance requiring EV-ready parking in new construction; clear regulatory requirement with percentages and building types.
